final_answer: ## Complete Solution: Photochemical Decomposition Rate of MB at 785 nm vs 532 nm

plan: Identify and list all given variables: laser intensity $I$, electromagnetic enhancement factor $\Sigma$, charge excitation cross-sections $\sigma_{785}$ and $\sigma_{532}$, and baseline (no-plasmon) decomposition rates $R_0$ at each wavelength.

State the rate model: the total photochemical decomposition rate is the sum of the direct (unenhanced) laser contribution and the plasmon-enhanced charge-excitation contribution: $R_\text{total} = R_0 + \Sigma \cdot \sigma \cdot I$.
Calculate the plasmon-enhanced contribution at 785 nm: $R_\text{plasmon}(785) = \Sigma \times \sigma_{785} \times I$.
Add the baseline rate to obtain $R_\text{total}(785)$; assess whether $R_0$ is negligible relative to the plasmon term.
Repeat steps 3–4 at 532 nm to obtain $R_\text{total}(532)$.
Compute the ratio $R_\text{total}(785) / R_\text{total}(532)$ to compare the two rates.

Interpret the physical meaning of the ratio in terms of the difference in cross-sections between the two wavelengths (since $\Sigma$ and $I$ are identical, the ratio reduces to $\sigma_{785}/\sigma_{532}$). --- ## Full Solution ### Step 1 — Gather variables | Symbol | Value | Notes | |---|---|---| | $I$ | $2.0 \times 10^{20}$ photons cm⁻² s⁻¹ | same for both wavelengths | | $\Sigma$ | $4 \times 10^{4}$ | same for both wavelengths | | $\sigma_{785}$ | $3 \times 10^{-18}$ cm² | charge excitation cross-section at 785 nm | | $\sigma_{532}$ | $1 \times 10^{-18}$ cm² | charge excitation cross-section at 532 nm | | $R_0(785)$ | $0.1$ molecules s⁻¹ | direct (no-plasmon) rate at 785 nm | | $R_0(532)$ | $0.02$ molecules s⁻¹ | direct (no-plasmon) rate at 532 nm | --- ### Step 2 — Rate model The electromagnetic enhancement factor $\Sigma$ amplifies the local photon flux seen by the molecule adsorbed near the nanocube. The rate of charge-excited photochemical events is proportional to this enhanced flux times the cross-section: $$R_\text{plasmon} = \Sigma \cdot \sigma \cdot I \quad \text{[molecules s}^{-1}\text{]}$$ The total decomposition rate (direct laser + plasmon channel) is: $$\boxed{R_\text{total} = R_0 + \Sigma \cdot \sigma \cdot I}$$ Because assumption 4 states that all photochemical activity is due to charge excitation (heating is negligible), the dominant term will be the plasmon-mediated one; $R_0$ represents the residual direct photolysis and will be retained for completeness. --- ### Step 3 — Plasmon-enhanced rate at 785 nm $$R_\text{plasmon}(785) = \Sigma \cdot \sigma_{785} \cdot I$$ $$= (4 \times 10^{4}) \times (3 \times 10^{-18}\ \text{cm}^2) \times (2.0 \times 10^{20}\ \text{photons cm}^{-2}\text{s}^{-1})$$ Collect the numerical prefactors and exponents separately: $$\text{Prefactor: } 4 \times 3 \times 2.0 = 24$$ $$\text{Exponent: } 10^{4} \times 10^{-18} \times 10^{20} = 10^{4-18+20} = 10^{6}$$ $$R_\text{plasmon}(785) = 24 \times 10^{6} = 2.4 \times 10^{7}\ \text{molecules s}^{-1}$$ --- ### Step 4 — Total rate at 785 nm $$R_\text{total}(785) = R_0(785) + R_\text{plasmon}(785)$$ $$= 0.1 + 2.4 \times 10^{7} \approx 2.4 \times 10^{7}\ \text{molecules s}^{-1}$$ The direct term ($0.1$ molecules s⁻¹) is eight orders of magnitude smaller than the plasmon term and is negligible; the plasmon channel completely dominates. --- ### Step 5 — Plasmon-enhanced rate at 532 nm $$R_\text{plasmon}(532) = \Sigma \cdot \sigma_{532} \cdot I$$ $$= (4 \times 10^{4}) \times (1 \times 10^{-18}\ \text{cm}^2) \times (2.0 \times 10^{20}\ \text{photons cm}^{-2}\text{s}^{-1})$$ $$\text{Prefactor: } 4 \times 1 \times 2.0 = 8$$ $$\text{Exponent: } 10^{4-18+20} = 10^{6}$$ $$R_\text{plasmon}(532) = 8 \times 10^{6} = 8.0 \times 10^{6}\ \text{molecules s}^{-1}$$ Total rate at 532 nm: $$R_\text{total}(532) = 0.02 + 8.0 \times 10^{6} \approx 8.0 \times 10^{6}\ \text{molecules s}^{-1}$$ Again the direct term is negligible. --- ### Step 6 — Comparison $$\frac{R_\text{total}(785)}{R_\text{total}(532)} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^{7}}{8.0 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{24}{8} = 3.0$$ --- ### Step 7 — Physical interpretation Since $\Sigma$ and $I$ are identical at both wavelengths, the ratio reduces exactly to the ratio of the charge-excitation cross-sections: $$\frac{R(785)}{R(532)} = \frac{\sigma_{785}}{\sigma_{532}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-18}}{1 \times 10^{-18}} = 3$$ This reflects the fact that at 785 nm the plasmonic near-field more efficiently drives hot-electron injection into MB (larger $\sigma$), likely because the plasmon resonance at 785 nm couples more effectively with the relevant electronic transitions in the MB–Ag system, even though 532 nm is also resonant. The 3× larger cross-section at 785 nm directly translates into a 3× faster decomposition rate under identical illumination conditions. --- ### Summary of Results | Wavelength | $R_\text{plasmon}$ | $R_0$ | $R_\text{total}$ | |---|---|---|---| | 785 nm | $2.4 \times 10^{7}$ mol s⁻¹ | $0.1$ mol s⁻¹ | $\mathbf{2.4 \times 10^{7}}$ mol s⁻¹ | | 532 nm | $8.0 \times 10^{6}$ mol s⁻¹ | $0.02$ mol s⁻¹ | $\mathbf{8.0 \times 10^{6}}$ mol s⁻¹ | **The 785 nm illumination decomposes MB at a rate 3× higher than 532 nm illumination**, driven entirely by the larger charge-excitation cross-section at the longer wavelength.

The ratio has already been computed in the plan: R_total(785) / R_total(532) = 2.4×10⁷ / 8.0×10⁶ = 3.0. No tool call needed.
